[chapter_12_craft_001] In the revised block beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:255, a cold reader can identify one false public persona and one concrete distortion of fact or quote before the scene advances to Layla's amplification or Roy's response. findings.problem: The chapter reproduces calibration-only style-bible example lines verbatim: Doug's "I drive a Civic," Roy's "I don't watch videos. I barely watch television," and Doug's "I bought better cereal." That is direct voice-calibration leakage rather than scene-generated speech, and it makes Doug and Roy sound preloaded instead of overheard.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the copied dialogue beats at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:11, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:13, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:125, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:137, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:229, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:233 with fresh wording generated from the style-bible voice rules, not from the example lines. Doug still needs to answer Alex's abstraction too literally and still needs to reduce fame to a grocery-level metric; Roy still needs to dismiss the video premise in his own plain register.

findings.problem: Once the profile is established as well-written and completely wrong, the chapter spends five more paragraphs paraphrasing slightly different versions of the same misreading. The turn lands by the "midwestern Everyman" paragraph, but the scene keeps re-proving it instead of moving into the sharper consequences with Layla, the office, and Roy.

findings.problem: Prose formula saturation. Chapter 12 (the Alex Chen profile) is the longest chapter in the novel and is heavily narrated throughout. The 'the way' comparison appears at high density: 'looked at the interior the way Doug had seen adjusters look at damaged property' (2886), 'found significance the way Doug's apartment found pollen' (3000). Negation-correction pairs accumulate in Doug's reaction to the profile: 'not a philosophy. It was structural.' (3042), 'not artlessly, but without reaching for beauty' (implied patterns throughout). The post-profile reflection section (3062-3124) is the worst — nearly every sentence in Doug's reaction to the article contains a corrective or qualifying clause. The 'which was' appositive appears ~14 times in the chapter. KEEP the pollen comparison at line 3000 — it is the chapter's strongest figure. REPLACE elsewhere.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2812-3126. The profile-reaction section (3062-3124) is the primary target. Doug's paragraph-by-paragraph correction of Alex's article (3066-3078) uses the same syntactic move repeatedly: 'The article said X. This was Y.' Vary the syntax — some corrections should be single sentences, some should be elliptical, some should just be Doug's thought without the article's frame. In the shadowing sections (2884-3060), cut at least half of the 'the way [noun] [verb]' constructions — Alex observing Doug doesn't need to be constantly compared to other modes of observation. The pollen comparison (3000) may stay. Aim for no more than 4 negation-correction pairs, 6 'which was' appositives, and 5 'the way' comparisons in the full chapter.

findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 2824 and 3044. The phrases 'I drive a Civic' and 'I bought better cereal' must not appear verbatim. Each replacement must serve the same function (Doug answering abstractly with concrete specifics) in Doug's voice: literal, precise, factual, slightly too detailed for the context.
findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Two of Doug's calibration-only lines appear verbatim in Chapter 12: 'I drive a Civic' at line 2824, and 'I bought better cereal' at line 3044. The style bible for doug_bridge lists both under example_lines with the 'CALIBRATION ONLY' header.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2824 and snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3044. For line 2824: replace Doug's deflection to the journalist's 'What drives you?' with a different Doug-specific literal misunderstanding — he could answer with a specific fact about his commute, his alarm clock, or his morning routine. The humor should come from the same source (treating an abstract question as a concrete one) without using the calibration phrasing. For line 3044: replace 'I bought better cereal' with a different specific, small change that carries the same weight — a different brand he switched to, a slightly upgraded item, something precise and Doug-specific that shows the change is real but tiny.

findings.acceptance_test: Re-read the revised Chapter 12. Total chapter length must be at least 40 lines shorter than current. Either the office scene or the Kroger scene must be significantly compressed (20+ lines shorter) or removed. The post-profile reflection (3062-3124) must contain no more than 2 article-vs-reality corrections (down from 5+). The chapter must still include: the apartment interview, the lunch with Roy (which is strong), and the Simone session. The Kroger 'cereal' exchange must use the revised (non-calibration) phrasing per F14.
findings.problem: Chapter-level sprawl. Chapter 12 (the Alex Chen profile day) is the novel's longest chapter and restates the same insight — that the public Doug is an invention and the real Doug is unremarkable — across four distinct scenes (the apartment interview, the office, the lunch with Roy, the Simone session) and then again in Doug's post-profile reaction (3062-3124). The office scene (2884-2924) and the Kroger grocery scene (3010-3058) add texture but no new dramatic turns: Diane calling Doug 'iconic,' a college kid asking for a photo, and Beverly the cashier are all variations on 'strangers see the internet Doug, not the real Doug,' which the apartment interview already established. The post-profile reflection (3062-3124) then states this insight explicitly for a fourth time. The chapter's dramatic work — showing the gap between the article's Doug and the real Doug — is complete by the end of the Simone session. Everything after that is restatement.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2812-3126. Cut or radically compress one of: the office scene (2884-2924) or the Kroger scene (3010-3058). The Kroger scene's college-kid photo is charming but adds no dramatic turn the office scene doesn't already deliver. If the Kroger scene is kept, the 'I bought better cereal' exchange (which must be replaced per F14 anyway) should carry the scene's only new beat. The post-profile reflection (3062-3124) must be trimmed by at least a third — Doug's paragraph-by-paragraph correction of the article (3066-3078) makes the same point repeatedly ('the article said X, the reality was Y'). Pick the 2 strongest corrections, cut the rest. The chapter should lose at least 40 lines overall.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's greatest risk is timidity. The premise — a man who is not funny trying to be funny — invites a safe, gently comic execution where Doug is lovably bumbling and everything works out. Resist this. Doug's failures should be genuinely uncomfortable to witness. His loneliness should be genuinely lonely, not charmingly solitary. The toast should contain things that are genuinely inappropriate, not safely edgy. The comedy should come from truth that hurts, not from situations that are merely awkward. The moments of tenderness — the Gloria chapter, the Kelsey lunch, the Simone conversation — should be earned through accumulated specificity, not through the narrative signaling that it's time to feel. Every chapter should contain at least one moment that a cautious writer would soften, and the drafting agent should not soften it.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is a craft failure. If Doug is drunk, describe what drunk looks and feels like — the specific sensory experience, not the label. If Doug is lonely, show the loneliness through the objects and routines that fill the space where other people would be — the cereal dinner, the second bedroom, the unpacked boxes. If a scene involves embarrassment, shame, failure, or longing, render it in full physical and emotional detail. The reader should feel it, not be told about it. Replace every euphemism with the specific thing it is hiding.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for most characters. Uncontracted forms should be reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific moments (Doug going precise under stress, Megan's composed register). If a line reads stiff and the character is not being deliberately formal, contract it.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should share the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-4), dialogue density (high-dialogue chapters followed by interior-heavy chapters), pacing (frenetic chapters followed by still ones), emotional register (comic escalation followed by tender quiet), narrative distance (very close interior followed by observed social scenes). The novel should feel like it breathes — tight chapters followed by loose ones, crowded chapters followed by solitary ones. Chapter 3 (quiet office day) must feel structurally different from Chapter 10 (quiet visit to Gloria). Chapter 9 (the corporate disaster) must feel structurally different from Chapter 14 (the tour), even though both involve performance failure. Achieve variety through scene construction, not through gimmick.
prose_style_profile.diction: Mid-register, clean, precise. No pyrotechnics. No extended metaphors. The prose should have the quality of someone describing what happened accurately — not artlessly, but without reaching for beauty. When beauty arrives, it should feel accidental, like Doug's humor: a precisely observed detail that turns out to be devastating not because the prose reached for devastation but because the detail was exact. Insurance language may surface in narration when it captures something emotional more precisely than emotional language would. Avoid lyrical flights, extended similes, or any construction that would sound wrong coming from a narrator who is sympathetic to Doug's plainness.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, tightly bound to Doug's perspective. The narrator has access to Doug's interior — his observations, his assessments, his emotional processing through objects and details — but not to any other character's thoughts. Other characters are rendered through Doug's perception: what they say, how they move, what Doug notices about them. The narrator's voice is distinct from Doug's voice but sympathetic to it — slightly more articulate, slightly more aware, but never mocking and never superior. The humor in the narration comes from the same source as Doug's humor: precision about mundane things that reveals the absurdity or sadness underneath. The narrator does not explain the comedy. The narrator does not wink.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied and controlled. Default to mid-length sentences with clean syntax. Use short sentences for comic timing — the flat declarative that follows an elaborate setup. Use longer, more syntactically complex sentences for interior passages where Doug is processing something he cannot name. Avoid uniform sentence length across paragraphs. The prose should breathe — alternating between compression and expansion, between observation and reflection, between the external world and Doug's interior. Comedy requires rhythm more than any other mode: the setup needs room, the punch needs precision, and the space after needs silence.
